title: Yellow Discharge from a Cat's Prepuce: Causes and Next Steps
meta_desc: Noticed yellow discharge from your cat's prepuce? Learn what causes it, why it is a medium-priority concern, and how photos help your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: Yellow discharge from a cat's penis typically indicates a bacterial infection requiring a veterinary exam within 24–48 hours, though inability to urinate is a life-threatening emergency.

What does yellow discharge from my cat's penis mean?

Yellow discharge from the prepuce (the skin covering the penis) is often a sign of inflammation or infection, medically known as balanoposthitis. While small amounts of clear or white discharge can be normal grooming residue, yellow or greenish fluid usually indicates the presence of white blood cells fighting a bacterial infection. This can be caused by trapped debris, local trauma, or underlying urinary tract issues.

Is yellow preputial discharge in cats a medical emergency?

  • Medium Urgency: In most cases, yellow discharge is not an immediate life-threatening emergency, but it indicates discomfort and an infection that requires a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Critical Emergency: If your cat is straining to urinate, crying in the litter box, or seems unable to pass urine, this is a urinary blockage and you must seek immediate emergency help.

How does taking a photo of the discharge help my veterinarian?

  • Preserves evidence: Cats are fastidious groomers and may lick away the discharge before you arrive at the clinic.
  • Detailed assessment: A clear, well-lit photo allows the vet to assess the color, thickness, and exact location of the fluid.
  • Effective triage: Visual evidence helps the veterinary team determine the severity of the inflammation and prioritize your cat's care.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Balanoposthitis, though rare in cats, can present with a mucopurulent preputial discharge. Diagnostic evaluation should include thorough examination of the penis and prepuce, up to the level of the fornix, to identify underlying predisposing factors. Saline infusion through a rigid endoscope can facilitate examination, but an otoscope can be used if necessary; sedation or general anesthesia may be required. Preputial cytology may be helpful, and bacterial cultures, though potentially difficult to interpret due to normal preputial flora, may help identify unusual organisms and determine antibiotic sensitivities for refractory cases. Treatment involves correcting any predisposing factors, clipping long hair away from the preputial orifice, and thoroughly flushing the preputial cavity with a mild antiseptic solution such as dilute povidone-iodine, chlorhexidine, or sterile saline.

Chapter: Urology, Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1401)

faqs.question: Should I try to clean the area myself?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid using any soaps, alcohols, or ointments on the area before your vet visit. These can cause further irritation to sensitive membranes and may interfere with diagnostic tests.

faqs.question: Can this be treated with over-the-counter medicine?
faqs.answer: No, yellow discharge usually requires prescription antibiotics or anti-inflammatory medications after a veterinarian has confirmed the cause through an exam or a swab.

faqs.question: What other symptoms should I look for?
faqs.answer: Watch for excessive licking of the genital area, blood in the urine, lethargy, or a decrease in appetite, as these provide more clues for your veterinarian.
